title: Alcohol and Violence: The Nature of the Relationship and the Promise of Prevention By Robert Nash Parker and Kevin J. McCaffree Lexington Books. 2013. 230 pages. $65 cloth
abstract: Does alcohol cause violence? From personal experience, I would say yes! The majority of physical altercations that I have been in during my life have involved alcohol (in fact, all of my illegal physical altercations have involved alcohol). However, was alcohol the cause of these violent outbursts (i.e., assaulting another person)? Setting personal experiences aside, it would appear that this causal relationship has some empirical validity since many studies show that alcohol was involved in nearly half of violent offenses in the United States (Roizen 1993). Despite this apparent causal relationship between alcohol and violence, researchers are still clearly undecided regarding the nature of this relationship (Parker and Auerhahn 1998). Given the difficulty of measuring violence (e.g., what constitutes violence?), measuring alcohol levels in offenders when a violent incident occurs (e.g., how do we get an accurate measure of a violent offender's blood alcohol level immediately after an offense?), and measuring the litany of other factors to rule out spuriousness, providing strong evidence of the causal nature of alcohol and violence appears to be a monumental task.
